title: Sources and Further Reading: Forest Wisdom
text_content:
Every claim in this episode is cited inline so you can check it yourself. Here is the full reference list in APA style. Use it to verify a fact, to dig deeper, or to start your own research for the project.

## References

Aidoo, A. A. (1988). Our sister killjoy. Longman.

Dutfield, G. (2006). Protecting traditional knowledge and folklore: A review of progress in diplomacy and policy formulation. International Centre for Trade and Sustainable Development.

Fortune Business Insights. (2024). Herbal medicine market size, share and COVID-19 impact analysis. https://www.fortunebusinessinsights.com

Grand View Research. (2024). Herbal medicine market size, share and trends analysis report. https://www.grandviewresearch.com

Gualinga, P. (2019). Statements on traditional knowledge and plant medicine.

Lewis, W. H., Kennelly, E. J., Bass, G. N., Wedner, H. J., Elvin-Lewis, M. P., & Fast, D. M. (2003). Ritualistic use of the holly Ilex guayusa by Amazonian Jivaro Indians. Journal of Ethnopharmacology, 33(1-2), 25-30.

Pendergrast, M. (2013). For God, country, and Coca-Cola: The definitive history of the great American soft drink (3rd ed.). Basic Books.

Scott, C. (2024). Biopiracy: Using new laws and databases to protect indigenous communities. Richmond Journal of Law and Technology, 30.

United Nations. (2007). United Nations Declaration on the Rights of Indigenous Peoples. UN General Assembly.

World Health Organization. (2023). Traditional medicine strategy 2014 to 2023. https://www.who.int

World Intellectual Property Organization. (2024). WIPO Treaty on Intellectual Property, Genetic Resources and Associated Traditional Knowledge. WIPO Diplomatic Conference, Geneva.

Akan oral tradition (proverbs as commonly recorded).

module_title: Episode 5: Guayusa & Kola Nut — Forest Wisdom
title: Knowledge Check: Forest Wisdom
text_content:
This check pulls from all four lenses in Episode 5: geography, social studies, economics, and language.

Each time you take it, you get 10 questions drawn from a larger pool, and the answer choices are shuffled, so a retake is never the same test twice. You need 80 percent to pass, and you can retake it as many times as you like. The goal is understanding, not gatekeeping.

After each question you see an explanation and a citation. Read them. The reasons a wrong answer is wrong are the best study material here.

When you finish, take the Project assignment for this episode, where the bigger questions, what it means that chemistry is relationship and who should benefit from traditional knowledge, get the long-form treatment they deserve.
quiz_content: {"passingScore":80,"attemptsAllowed":-1,"questionsPerAttempt":10,"shuffleOptions":true,"questions":[{"id":"q1","questionText":"What are guayusa and the kola nut?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q1a","text":"An Amazon holly brewed as tea, and a West African rainforest seed that is chewed"},{"id":"q1b","text":"Two kinds of coffee"},{"id":"q1c","text":"Two desert cacti"},{"id":"q1d","text":"Two ocean plants"}],"correctOptionId":"q1a","explanation":"Guayusa (Ilex guayusa) is an Amazon holly brewed into a pre-dawn tea; the kola nut is a chewed seed from the West African rainforest. Both are forest plants.","citation":"Lesson: Two Plants of the Forest"},{"id":"q2","questionText":"What happens to guayusa's chemistry when it is grown outside intact forest?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q2a","text":"It loses about half of its active compounds"},{"id":"q2b","text":"It becomes twice as strong"},{"id":"q2c","text":"Nothing changes"},{"id":"q2d","text":"It turns poisonous"}],"correctOptionId":"q2a","explanation":"Guayusa loses about 50 percent of its active chemistry outside intact forest, because its strength depends on the surrounding ecosystem. For these plants, chemistry is relationship.","citation":"Lesson: Chemistry Is Relationship"},{"id":"q3","questionText":"What does it mean to say that for guayusa and kola, chemistry is relationship?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q3a","text":"Their strength depends on the forest ecosystem around them, not the plant alone"},{"id":"q3b","text":"They only grow when two plants are married"},{"id":"q3c","text":"Their chemistry never changes anywhere"},{"id":"q3d","text":"They have no active compounds"}],"correctOptionId":"q3a","explanation":"Their active chemistry is partly produced by their relationship to the forest, the shade, humidity, soil fungi, and companion plants. Take them out of that web and the chemistry fades.","citation":"Lesson: Chemistry Is Relationship"},{"id":"q4","questionText":"What is agroforestry, the right way to grow these plants?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q4a","text":"A forest garden, growing the crop among many species under a real canopy"},{"id":"q4b","text":"Clearing the forest for open fields"},{"id":"q4c","text":"Growing the plant indoors"},{"id":"q4d","text":"A single-crop plantation"}],"correctOptionId":"q4a","explanation":"Agroforestry is a forest garden, the opposite of a cleared monocrop plantation. For these forest plants, keeping the forest is the farming.","citation":"Lesson: Chemistry Is Relationship"},{"id":"q5","questionText":"What is the main threat to guayusa forest in Ecuador?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q5a","text":"Cattle ranching"},{"id":"q5b","text":"Palm-oil plantations"},{"id":"q5c","text":"Coffee farming"},{"id":"q5d","text":"Too much rain"}],"correctOptionId":"q5a","explanation":"Cattle ranching has cleared much of the guayusa forest in the Ecuadorian Amazon. In West Africa, by contrast, palm-oil plantations are the main threat to kola forest.","citation":"Lesson: Forests Under Threat"},{"id":"q6","questionText":"How is much of this forest being protected?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q6a","text":"Indigenous land rights, like the Achuar territory, and West African sacred groves"},{"id":"q6b","text":"By clearing it for safer crops"},{"id":"q6c","text":"By fencing it off from all people"},{"id":"q6d","text":"It is not protected at all"}],"correctOptionId":"q6a","explanation":"The Achuar protect around 700,000 hectares through Indigenous land rights, and West African sacred groves protect tens of thousands of forest patches. The people who hold the knowledge are protecting the forest.","citation":"Lesson: Forests Under Threat"},{"id":"q7","questionText":"What is the Achuar Wayus Aramu?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q7a","text":"A pre-dawn guayusa ceremony that has been a community institution for over a thousand years"},{"id":"q7b","text":"A modern energy-drink brand"},{"id":"q7c","text":"A type of forest tree"},{"id":"q7d","text":"A trade tax"}],"correctOptionId":"q7a","explanation":"The Wayus Aramu is the Achuar pre-dawn guayusa ceremony, a community institution documented in oral tradition for over 1,500 years, where families gather before dawn to drink guayusa and share dreams.","citation":"Lesson: The Pre-Dawn Cup and the Kola Road"},{"id":"q8","questionText":"How did the West African kola trade work across the Sahara?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q8a","text":"Forest kola was carried over a thousand miles by camel to hubs like Timbuktu, gaining a large markup"},{"id":"q8b","text":"Kola was shipped by sea to Europe only"},{"id":"q8c","text":"Kola was never traded outside its forest"},{"id":"q8d","text":"Kola was flown north by plane"}],"correctOptionId":"q8a","explanation":"Forest kola traveled north by camel caravan to trans-Saharan hubs like Timbuktu, Djenne, and Gao, gaining about a 200 percent markup at intermediate markets and 400 percent at the destination.","citation":"Lesson: The Pre-Dawn Cup and the Kola Road"},{"id":"q9","questionText":"What is a reciprocity economy, as seen with guayusa and kola?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q9a","text":"Exchange based on long-term relationships and mutual obligation rather than cash"},{"id":"q9b","text":"A stock market for plants"},{"id":"q9c","text":"A system with no exchange at all"},{"id":"q9d","text":"Paying only in gold"}],"correctOptionId":"q9a","explanation":"A reciprocity economy is built on long-term relationships and mutual obligation, not cash. The Achuar guayusa and West African kola economies both rested on social bonds.","citation":"Lesson: The Reciprocity Economy"},{"id":"q10","questionText":"What famous product did John Pemberton create in 1886 using kola extract?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q10a","text":"Coca-Cola"},{"id":"q10b","text":"Pepsi"},{"id":"q10c","text":"Coffee"},{"id":"q10d","text":"Tea"}],"correctOptionId":"q10a","explanation":"In 1886, John Pemberton created Coca-Cola using kola extract, commercializing West African traditional knowledge with no consent or benefit to the originating communities.","citation":"Lesson: How Coca-Cola Got Its Name"},{"id":"q11","questionText":"What is biopiracy?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q11a","text":"Commercializing traditional knowledge without consent or compensation for the originating community"},{"id":"q11b","text":"Stealing ships at sea"},{"id":"q11c","text":"Growing plants illegally"},{"id":"q11d","text":"A type of fair-trade certification"}],"correctOptionId":"q11a","explanation":"Biopiracy is when traditional knowledge becomes a corporate product and the returns flow past the originating community, with no recognition or compensation. The term was coined in 1993.","citation":"Lesson: How Coca-Cola Got Its Name"},{"id":"q12","questionText":"What did the 2024 WIPO Treaty achieve?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q12a","text":"It was the first binding international anti-biopiracy agreement"},{"id":"q12b","text":"It banned all traditional medicine"},{"id":"q12c","text":"It made Coca-Cola illegal"},{"id":"q12d","text":"It had no effect"}],"correctOptionId":"q12a","explanation":"The 2024 WIPO Treaty on Intellectual Property, Genetic Resources and Traditional Knowledge was the first binding international anti-biopiracy agreement, with 30 signatories.","citation":"Lesson: Whose Knowledge Is It"},{"id":"q13","questionText":"What does FPIC stand for, as a standard for working with Indigenous communities?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q13a","text":"Free, Prior, and Informed Consent"},{"id":"q13b","text":"Forest Protection and International Conservation"},{"id":"q13c","text":"Fair Price for Indigenous Crops"},{"id":"q13d","text":"Federal Plant Import Code"}],"correctOptionId":"q13a","explanation":"FPIC is Free, Prior, and Informed Consent: a community must be free to refuse, informed in advance, and able to give or withhold consent as a community.","citation":"Lesson: Whose Knowledge Is It"},{"id":"q14","questionText":"About how large is the global traditional-medicine market?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q14a","text":"Estimates range from about 70 billion to 233 billion dollars a year"},{"id":"q14b","text":"About 1 million dollars a year"},{"id":"q14c","text":"Exactly 10 billion dollars a year"},{"id":"q14d","text":"It has no measurable value"}],"correctOptionId":"q14a","explanation":"Estimates of the traditional-medicine market range from about 70 billion to 233 billion dollars a year, depending on how it is defined. The WHO estimates about 80 percent of people in developing nations use plant-based medicines as primary care.","citation":"Lesson: Biopiracy and Benefit-Sharing"},{"id":"q15","questionText":"What does a benefit-sharing model do for communities?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q15a","text":"It pays growers more than the commodity rate and requires consent and attribution"},{"id":"q15b","text":"It removes the community from the trade"},{"id":"q15c","text":"It pays nothing extra"},{"id":"q15d","text":"It bans community processing"}],"correctOptionId":"q15a","explanation":"Benefit-sharing models pay growers several times the commodity rate, require consent and attribution, and share revenue, treating the relationship as a partnership rather than extraction.","citation":"Lesson: Biopiracy and Benefit-Sharing"},{"id":"q16","questionText":"How does value-added processing help these communities?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q16a","text":"Processing raw material themselves, like making guayusa tea or extract, captures the margin extraction removed"},{"id":"q16b","text":"It lowers the value of their crop"},{"id":"q16c","text":"It sends all profit overseas"},{"id":"q16d","text":"It requires clearing the forest"}],"correctOptionId":"q16a","explanation":"When a community processes its own raw material rather than exporting it unprocessed, it captures the value-added margin that extraction economics historically removed.","citation":"Lesson: Biopiracy and Benefit-Sharing"},{"id":"q17","questionText":"In the Achuar creation narrative, how does guayusa come to be?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q17a","text":"Divine tears in the darkness become the first guayusa trees, and humans awaken through dreams"},{"id":"q17b","text":"It was bought from a market"},{"id":"q17c","text":"It fell from a passing ship"},{"id":"q17d","text":"It was invented by scientists"}],"correctOptionId":"q17a","explanation":"In the Achuar narrative, darkness gives way to divine tears that become the first guayusa trees, and people awaken through dreams. Dreams are treated as a knowledge technology and the plant as a bridge between worlds.","citation":"Lesson: Two Ways of Knowing"},{"id":"q18","questionText":"What does Patricia Gualinga mean by saying Wayusa is relationship, not just caffeine?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q18a","text":"She rejects the Western scientific frame rather than arguing inside it, a framework rejection"},{"id":"q18b","text":"She says the plant has no effect"},{"id":"q18c","text":"She agrees the plant is only its chemicals"},{"id":"q18d","text":"She is describing a recipe"}],"correctOptionId":"q18a","explanation":"By saying wayusa is relationship, Gualinga does not argue inside Western science but dissolves its categories. It is a rhetorical move called framework rejection, asserting another valid way of knowing.","citation":"Lesson: Two Ways of Knowing"},{"id":"q19","questionText":"What is the literary power of an Akan proverb like 'the bitter kola teaches what sweet words cannot'?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q19a","text":"Proverbial compression: maximum meaning in minimum words, using an everyday image as philosophy"},{"id":"q19b","text":"It is a long, detailed story"},{"id":"q19c","text":"It has no figurative meaning"},{"id":"q19d","text":"It is a modern advertising slogan"}],"correctOptionId":"q19a","explanation":"Akan proverbs pack philosophy into a few words, using agricultural images as ideas. The bitter kola standing for hard-won strength is proverbial compression at work.","citation":"Lesson: The Wisdom of Proverbs"},{"id":"q20","questionText":"In Ama Ata Aidoo's writing, what is Coca-Cola reduced to?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q20a","text":"Water pretending to be wisdom"},{"id":"q20b","text":"The finest drink ever made"},{"id":"q20c","text":"A sacred medicine"},{"id":"q20d","text":"A traditional kola ceremony"}],"correctOptionId":"q20a","explanation":"Aidoo reduces Coca-Cola to water pretending to be wisdom, using contrast and diminishment, and a grandmother's laughter as dismissal, to critique cultural appropriation.","citation":"Lesson: Water Pretending to Be Wisdom"},{"id":"q21","questionText":"Where does guayusa grow?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q21a","text":"The Amazon cloud forest of Ecuador, Peru, and southern Colombia"},{"id":"q21b","text":"The deserts of North Africa"},{"id":"q21c","text":"The mountains of Japan"},{"id":"q21d","text":"The plains of North America"}],"correctOptionId":"q21a","explanation":"Guayusa is an Amazon holly from the cloud-forest transition zone where the Andes meet the Amazon, in Ecuador, Peru, and southern Colombia.","citation":"Lesson: Two Plants of the Forest"},{"id":"q22","questionText":"What is one ecosystem service a living forest provides?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q22a","text":"Lower temperature extremes, humidity, rainfall recycling, carbon storage, and soil building"},{"id":"q22b","text":"Nothing of economic value"},{"id":"q22c","text":"Only timber"},{"id":"q22d","text":"Faster crop spoilage"}],"correctOptionId":"q22a","explanation":"Ecosystem services are the benefits a living forest provides: cooling, humidity, rainfall recycling, carbon storage, and soil building. None of it shows on a price tag, but all of it is real value.","citation":"Lesson: Chemistry Is Relationship"},{"id":"q23","questionText":"What roles did the kola nut play in West African society?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q23a","text":"Currency for large transactions, plus ceremonial, diplomatic, and medicinal uses"},{"id":"q23b","text":"Only a snack food"},{"id":"q23c","text":"A building material"},{"id":"q23d","text":"It had no social role"}],"correctOptionId":"q23a","explanation":"Kola served as currency for large transactions and held ceremonial, diplomatic, and medicinal roles, with social bonds underpinning the economic relationships.","citation":"Lesson: The Pre-Dawn Cup and the Kola Road"},{"id":"q24","questionText":"What is social capital, in the economics of these plants?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q24a","text":"Non-monetary value from trust, reciprocity, and community bonds, like kola ceremonies creating alliances"},{"id":"q24b","text":"Money held in a bank"},{"id":"q24c","text":"The value of farm machinery"},{"id":"q24d","text":"A government subsidy"}],"correctOptionId":"q24a","explanation":"Social capital is the non-monetary value created by trust, reciprocity, and community bonds. Kola ceremonies, for example, built the alliances that underpinned trade.","citation":"Lesson: The Reciprocity Economy"},{"id":"q25","questionText":"What did the 2007 UN Declaration on the Rights of Indigenous Peoples do?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q25a","text":"It recognized Indigenous peoples' rights, including rights to their traditional knowledge"},{"id":"q25b","text":"It banned Indigenous farming"},{"id":"q25c","text":"It gave corporations ownership of plants"},{"id":"q25d","text":"It had nothing to do with knowledge"}],"correctOptionId":"q25a","explanation":"The 2007 UN Declaration recognized Indigenous peoples' rights, including the right to control their traditional knowledge, an international step toward addressing biopiracy.","citation":"Lesson: Whose Knowledge Is It"},{"id":"q26","questionText":"Why does keeping the forest standing matter for these plants in particular?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q26a","text":"The plant, its chemistry, and the knowledge are one system; lose the forest and you lose all three"},{"id":"q26b","text":"The forest has no effect on the plants"},{"id":"q26c","text":"The plants grow better in cleared fields"},{"id":"q26d","text":"The forest only matters for tourism"}],"correctOptionId":"q26a","explanation":"Because chemistry is relationship, protecting the forest protects the plant, its chemistry, and the traditional knowledge together. Lose the forest and all three are lost.","citation":"Lesson: Forests Under Threat"},{"id":"q27","questionText":"In the Achuar guayusa economy, what might a 1 kilogram bundle of dried guayusa be exchanged for?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q27a","text":"About 2 clay pots or 1 machete"},{"id":"q27b","text":"A car"},{"id":"q27c","text":"A gold bar"},{"id":"q27d","text":"Nothing; it was never traded"}],"correctOptionId":"q27a","explanation":"In the reciprocity economy, a 1 kilogram bundle of dried guayusa might be exchanged for about 2 clay pots or 1 machete, with the exchange building long-term relationships rather than maximizing profit.","citation":"Lesson: The Pre-Dawn Cup and the Kola Road"},{"id":"q28","questionText":"What is the biopiracy pattern, step by step?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q28a","text":"Traditional knowledge becomes corporate research, then a patented product, and the returns flow past the community"},{"id":"q28b","text":"A community sells its product at a fair price"},{"id":"q28c","text":"A government funds Indigenous research"},{"id":"q28d","text":"A plant goes extinct naturally"}],"correctOptionId":"q28a","explanation":"The pattern runs from traditional knowledge to corporate research and development, to a patent or trademark, to a global product, with the returns flowing past the originating community.","citation":"Lesson: Biopiracy and Benefit-Sharing"},{"id":"q29","questionText":"What does the WHO estimate about plant-based medicine in developing nations?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q29a","text":"About 80 percent of people use plant-based medicines as primary healthcare"},{"id":"q29b","text":"Almost no one uses them"},{"id":"q29c","text":"Only doctors use them"},{"id":"q29d","text":"They are banned everywhere"}],"correctOptionId":"q29a","explanation":"The WHO estimates about 80 percent of people in developing nations rely on plant-based medicines as their primary healthcare, which is part of why this market and its knowledge matter so much.","citation":"Lesson: Biopiracy and Benefit-Sharing"},{"id":"q30","questionText":"What is the literary effect of Aidoo's triple negation, that real kola 'cannot be bottled, cannot be sweetened, cannot be separated'?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q30a","text":"Repetition builds a post-colonial critique, insisting the tradition resists being turned into a product"},{"id":"q30b","text":"It is a recipe for making cola"},{"id":"q30c","text":"It praises the soft-drink industry"},{"id":"q30d","text":"It has no rhetorical purpose"}],"correctOptionId":"q30a","explanation":"The triple negation uses repetition to insist that the real tradition cannot be commercialized, building a post-colonial critique through rhythm and contrast.","citation":"Lesson: Water Pretending to Be Wisdom"},{"id":"q31","questionText":"What does the Akan proverb 'he who shares kola with his enemy shares the possibility of peace' show?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q31a","text":"That a kola ceremony can be a political act, a step toward reconciliation"},{"id":"q31b","text":"That kola is only a snack"},{"id":"q31c","text":"That enemies should never meet"},{"id":"q31d","text":"That kola has no meaning"}],"correctOptionId":"q31a","explanation":"The proverb shows kola as more than food: sharing it with an enemy is a political and diplomatic act that opens the possibility of peace.","citation":"Lesson: The Wisdom of Proverbs"},{"id":"q32","questionText":"What is knowledge sovereignty?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q32a","text":"A community's right to control how its traditional knowledge is used and shared"},{"id":"q32b","text":"A government owning all knowledge"},{"id":"q32c","text":"Making all knowledge free to corporations"},{"id":"q32d","text":"A type of patent for companies"}],"correctOptionId":"q32a","explanation":"Knowledge sovereignty is a community's right to control how its traditional knowledge enters markets and is used, one of the standards behind FPIC and the anti-biopiracy treaties.","citation":"Lesson: Whose Knowledge Is It"},{"id":"q33","questionText":"What is the main threat to kola forest in West Africa?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q33a","text":"Palm-oil plantations"},{"id":"q33b","text":"Cattle ranching"},{"id":"q33c","text":"Tea farming"},{"id":"q33d","text":"Snow"}],"correctOptionId":"q33a","explanation":"Palm-oil plantations have replaced much of the rainforest that kola needs, especially in Nigeria. Cattle ranching, by contrast, is the main threat to guayusa forest in Ecuador.","citation":"Lesson: Forests Under Threat"},{"id":"q34","questionText":"What is a gift economy?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q34a","text":"An economic system based on giving rather than market exchange, like sharing guayusa within family networks"},{"id":"q34b","text":"A store that only sells gifts"},{"id":"q34c","text":"A tax on presents"},{"id":"q34d","text":"Buying everything with cash"}],"correctOptionId":"q34a","explanation":"A gift economy is based on giving rather than buying and selling. Sharing guayusa within family networks is one example of the non-cash systems these plants lived inside.","citation":"Lesson: The Reciprocity Economy"},{"id":"q35","questionText":"Why is this episode different from the earlier commodity episodes?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q35a","text":"It is about two lesser-known forest plants and the bigger question of who owns traditional knowledge"},{"id":"q35b","text":"It is about a single global commodity like coffee"},{"id":"q35c","text":"It has nothing to do with plants"},{"id":"q35d","text":"It is only about chemistry"}],"correctOptionId":"q35a","explanation":"Rather than one global commodity, Episode 5 looks at two forest plants, guayusa and kola, and the question that runs through them: who has the right to use and benefit from traditional knowledge.","citation":"Lesson: Forest Wisdom: Guayusa and the Kola Nut"},{"id":"q36","questionText":"What was the Coca-Cola case an example of, in economic terms?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q36a","text":"Biopiracy: traditional kola knowledge became a global product with no benefit-sharing"},{"id":"q36b","text":"A fair benefit-sharing partnership"},{"id":"q36c","text":"A community-owned business"},{"id":"q36d","text":"A government program"}],"correctOptionId":"q36a","explanation":"The Coca-Cola case is a classic biopiracy example: West African kola knowledge became an American beverage and a global brand, with zero benefit-sharing with the originating communities.","citation":"Lesson: How Coca-Cola Got Its Name"}]}
